title: Proposition Chunking
one_liner: Use an LLM to decompose documents into atomic, self-contained factual propositions, quality-grade them with a second LLM pass, and index the surviving propositions in a vector store for fine-grained retrieval.
problem_solved: Conventional fixed-size chunks bundle many facts together, so the embedding of a chunk is a blurry average of multiple topics and retrieved chunks carry irrelevant surrounding text. Fine-grained factual queries (who/when/what) either miss the buried fact or drag in noise. Proposition chunking re-indexes the corpus as minimal, decontextualized factual statements so retrieval returns exactly the fact needed.
failure_modes: Specific factual questions retrieve long chunks where the answer is buried among unrelated sentences
Retrieved chunks pollute the context window with irrelevant text, diluting or distracting the generator
Chunk embeddings average multiple topics, so precise queries match poorly against multi-topic chunks
Retrieved text is uninterpretable in isolation because of pronouns and ambiguous references ('he', 'the company') that lost their antecedent at chunk boundaries
Answers to simple factoid queries are verbose or imprecise because the context handed to the LLM is much broader than the question
when_to_use: Your workload is dominated by short factoid queries (who/when/what/which) against fact-dense prose such as reports, wikis, encyclopedic or news-like content
You need highly precise, minimal context per retrieval hit rather than broad passages (e.g., strict token budgets, or downstream extraction pipelines)
Retrieved chunks keep containing the right region of text but the answer is diluted by surrounding material
Your corpus is small-to-medium and static enough that a per-chunk plus per-proposition LLM indexing pass is affordable
You want a quality gate on index content: each proposition is LLM-graded on accuracy/clarity/completeness/conciseness before being embedded
tradeoffs: Expensive index build: one LLM call per base chunk to generate propositions plus one LLM call per proposition to grade it; cost and build time scale with corpus size far worse than plain chunking
Index size grows: each chunk explodes into many propositions, multiplying embedding calls and vector count

Low contextual richness: propositions strip narrative flow, so questions needing surrounding context, synthesis, summarization, or multi-hop reasoning across a passage do worse than with larger chunks (the notebook's own comparison concedes this)

Proposition generation is model-dependent and can hallucinate, mis-split, or omit facts; the quality gate discards borderline propositions, silently losing recall (the notebook shows real propositions failing thresholds)
Not suited to tabular, structured, or visual content; assumes declarative factual prose

Avoid when queries are open-ended/analytical, when the corpus updates frequently (re-propositionizing churn), or when you cannot afford LLM-in-the-loop indexing; consider retrieving propositions but expanding to their parent chunk (chunk_id metadata) if the generator needs context
how_it_works: Indexing-time pipeline (spans chunking and indexing; retrieval itself is standard similarity search): (1) split the document into ~200-token base chunks with RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter.from_tiktoken_encoder (overlap 50) and tag each with a chunk_id; (2) for each chunk, invoke a ChatGroq LLM (llama-3.1-70b-versatile, temperature 0) with a few-shot prompt (FewShotChatMessagePromptTemplate) and structured output bound to a Pydantic GeneratePropositions model, yielding a list of atomic statements that each express a single fact, resolve pronouns to full names, include dates/qualifiers, and stand alone without context; (3) grade every proposition against its source chunk with a second structured-output LLM chain (GradePropositions) scoring accuracy, clarity, completeness, and conciseness 1-10, keeping only propositions scoring >=7 on all four; (4) embed surviving propositions (OllamaEmbeddings, nomic-embed-text:v1.5) into a FAISS vector store and expose it as a k=4 similarity retriever, each proposition carrying its parent chunk_id in metadata. The notebook then builds a parallel FAISS store over the raw 200-token chunks and runs three test queries against both, showing proposition retrieval returns pinpoint facts while chunk retrieval returns broader but noisier passages; a closing comparison table summarizes precision-vs-context tradeoffs.
key_code_signals: class GeneratePropositions(BaseModel) with propositions: List[str] — structured-output schema for atomic fact extraction
FewShotChatMessagePromptTemplate with a Neil Armstrong document→propositions example
System prompt enforcing: single fact, self-contained, full names not pronouns, dates/qualifiers, one subject-predicate relationship
class GradePropositions(BaseModel) scoring accuracy/clarity/completeness/conciseness 1-10 with thresholds = {each: 7} and passes_quality_check()
RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter.from_tiktoken_encoder(chunk_size=200, chunk_overlap=50) producing base chunks with chunk_id metadata
FAISS.from_documents(evaluated_propositions, embedding_model) vs FAISS.from_documents(doc_splits, embedding_model) — side-by-side proposition vs chunk retrievers, both k=4
related_techniques: choose_chunk_size: alternative — tunes fixed-size chunking instead of re-representing content as atomic facts
semantic_chunking: alternative — splits on semantic boundaries but keeps original text rather than rewriting it
contextual_chunk_headers: complementary-opposite — adds context to chunks, whereas propositions strip content down to atoms; useful counterweight
context_enrichment_window_around_chunk: complementary — the chunk_id metadata on each proposition enables expanding a hit back to its parent/neighboring chunks to restore lost context
HyPE_Hypothetical_Prompt_Embeddings: related — both spend LLM calls at index time to create retrieval-friendlier representations of content
reliable_rag: related pattern — reuses the LLM-as-grader structured-output pattern, here applied to index content instead of retrieved docs
raptor: alternative index-time LLM transformation — hierarchical summaries (coarser) vs propositions (finer)
simple_rag: refines — drop-in replacement for the naive chunk-embed-retrieve indexing step
summary: Proposition chunking replaces raw text chunks with LLM-generated atomic facts as the unit of indexing and retrieval. The problem: fixed-size chunks mix many facts, so embeddings blur topics, factoid queries miss buried answers, and retrieved passages carry irrelevant text and unresolved pronouns into the context window. The method: split documents into ~200-token base chunks, then use a structured-output LLM (ChatGroq llama-3.1-70b, few-shot prompted) to rewrite each chunk as self-contained propositions — one fact each, full entity names instead of pronouns, dates and qualifiers included. A second LLM grades every proposition 1-10 on accuracy, clarity, completeness, and conciseness; only propositions scoring at least 7 on all four are embedded (Ollama nomic-embed-text) into a FAISS store, each keeping its parent chunk_id. Side-by-side tests against a larger-chunk retriever show propositions win on precision and brevity for factual who/when/what queries, while larger chunks win on contextual richness and complex analytical questions. Costs: an LLM call per chunk plus per proposition at index time, a much larger vector count, and loss of narrative context.
notes_for_chunking: Structure is clean and pipeline-ordered. Cell 0 is a book-promotion markdown cell (strip). Cell 2 is one giant self-contained markdown cell holding Overview, Key Components, Motivation, Method Details, Benefits, Implementation, and Summary under H3 headers plus an reference to ../images/proposition_chunking.svg — it duplicates much of what the code shows, so consider splitting it by H3 or deduplicating against code sections. Cell 4 is a pip install (strip). Subsequent H3 markdown headers (Chunking, Generate Propositions, Quality Check, Embedding propositions in a vectorstore, Comparing performance with larger chunks size, Testing with H4 Test-1/2/3) map one-to-one onto the following code cells, making header-anchored chunking reliable. Code cells are medium-sized with meaningful inline comments; two contain long embedded prompt strings (proposition-generation system prompt, evaluation prompt) that are high-value content, not boilerplate. Outputs are short printed retrieval results and OllamaEmbeddings progress bars — the retrieval printouts are useful evidence of the precision-vs-context contrast, progress bars are junk. The final markdown comparison table (proposition vs simple chunk retrieval across nine aspects) is the densest takeaway cell; the last cell is a tracking-pixel image (strip). No base64 blobs.
